turns.text: Hello Herminia, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're adjusting to life after having your baby, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

Thank you. It’s strange to be the one answering the questions. Usually, I’m the one sitting on the other side of the exam table, reassuring a mother that what she’s feeling is normal, handing out pamphlets, and checking the clock to make sure I’m moving through the appointment efficiently. 

But sitting here now, with the baby finally asleep in the bassinet and my own body still feeling like it’s put itself back together with spare parts, the perspective is... different. It’s humbling, honestly. Everyone keeps telling me I’m "low-risk," that I know the drill because this is my second, and that I’m a professional. My midwife is kind, but she moves through my check-ups so quickly because she assumes I’ve got it all handled. And I *do* know the science—I know the anatomy of a latch, I know the weight percentiles—but knowing the science doesn’t make my back ache any less or stop the panic when the baby doesn’t wake up for a feed.

I find myself holding back my fears because I feel like I should know better. I’m the expert, right? I shouldn't be the one struggling to keep my head above water. It’s a very quiet, lonely kind of shame to have all the knowledge in your head but feel completely incapable in your own living room.
When you think about your goals for recovery and support in the days and weeks after birth, what did you most want or need — and how well did the postnatal care you received address those needs?

Honestly? What I wanted most was for someone to stop looking at my charts and start looking at *me*. During those appointments, there was this unspoken assumption that because I work in this field and because I’d "survived" the first round of motherhood, I didn't need the hand-holding. They’d check my vitals, ask if I was healing well, and assume that because the clinical boxes were ticked, I was doing just fine.

But I was drowning in the transition. I didn’t need a clinical review; I needed someone to sit with me and say, "It’s okay that you’re exhausted, and it’s okay that you’re scared you’re missing something." I felt like I had to maintain this facade of the "competent professional" because I was afraid that if I admitted I was struggling, it would somehow invalidate my expertise. 

The care I received was perfectly adequate by the medical standards—I was physically healthy, the baby was hitting her markers—but it was hollow. It felt like I was being treated as a peer rather than a patient. I didn't need a colleague; I needed a caregiver. I wish there had been more space for me to be vulnerable, to admit that even with all my training, I felt entirely lost at 3:00 AM.
When you think about feeding your baby — whether through breastfeeding, formula, or a combination — what motivated your choices, and how supported did you feel in pursuing the approach you wanted?

Breastfeeding feels like this sacred, non-negotiable goal I set for myself, largely because I spend my professional days advocating for it. I know the benefits, the immunology, the bonding—it’s all there in my brain. But when I’m sitting on the sofa, nursing my daughter, and she’s too tired to keep pulling, that clinical knowledge starts to feel like a trap. It turns into this internal standard I feel I *must* meet to prove I’m a "good" mother and a "good" provider.

I didn't really feel "supported" in the way I needed because no one asked me how it felt; they just asked if it was "working." Because I’m a nurse, there’s an assumption that if it’s not working, I’ll just fix it—I’ll look up the position, I’ll adjust the latch, I’ll troubleshoot the flow. But when it’s your own skin that’s cracked and bleeding, and your own nerves that are frayed, "troubleshooting" is the last thing you want to do. You want someone to hold the space for the frustration.

I felt like I was being watched by a silent, judgmental panel of my own professional peers. I was so worried about appearing incompetent that I didn’t even tell my midwife about the panic spikes I was having during feeds. I just masked it, nodded when she asked if we were "getting enough ounces," and kept pushing through the pain.

When you think about your emotional and mental health in the weeks and months after birth — the adjustment, the challenges, and the moments of wellbeing — what stands out most, and how well-supported did you feel?

What stands out is the silence—the way the house sounds when my husband heads off to work and it’s just me and this tiny, demanding life. There’s a specific kind of whiplash that comes with this. One day I’m standing in a clinic answering a worried mother’s questions with calm, practiced authority, and the next, I’m weeping over a pile of unfolded laundry because I feel like I’m failing at the most basic tasks.

The emotional support felt… distant. It’s almost as if my professional background acted as a shield that kept people from checking on my heart. Because I can explain postpartum hormone fluctuations or the neurobiology of infant sleep, people assume I’m insulated from the weight of it. It’s like they think if you understand the "why" of a symptom, you’re immune to the "how it feels." 

I didn't need a lecture on the "baby blues"; I needed someone to come over, hold the baby, and tell me I was doing a good job—not as a nurse, but as a mom. I feel this immense pressure to be the gold standard, and that keeps me from reaching out when I’m actually, truly struggling. I’ve felt very alone in my own head, guarding this secret that the "expert" is just as terrified as the patient.

When you think about the shift in your identity and sense of self after becoming a parent, what tensions or changes were most significant — and how did those changes interact with your other roles, such as partner, professional, or daughter?

The most significant tension is this fractured self I’m carrying around. I used to be able to compartmentalize—I had my "work self," which was steady, rational, and clear-eyed, and my "home self." But having a second baby has blurred those lines into a messy, exhausting smear. I’m thirty-six, and I feel like I’m constantly mourning the version of myself that had it all together.

At work, I’m the one people look to for answers. When I walk into a room, I put on that mantle of capability; I check the vitals, I offer the advice, and I feel like a pillar of support. But then I come home, and the "pillar" is struggling to find the energy to wash a bottle. There’s a sharp, stinging cognitive dissonance there. I look at my husband—who is wonderful, truly—and I feel a weird resentment that he gets to leave for his world, where he is just a professional, while I am anchored to this nursery, feeling like my entire existence has been reduced to my utility as a food source and a caretaker.

It’s hard to be the "expert" who needs help. It makes me feel like a fraud. I’m constantly analyzing my own mothering through the clinical lens I use at work, which is a miserable way to live. I’m judging my own progress, my own recovery, and my own daughter’s milestones, and it’s stripping the joy right out of the room. I don’t want to be a clinician right now; I just want to be allowed to be a tired woman who doesn't have the answers.
Can you describe a moment when you felt the tension between your new parental identity and another part of your identity most acutely?

It happened last Tuesday, during a routine check-up for my daughter. I was sitting on that crinkly paper, holding her, and my colleague—someone I’ve worked with for years—started rattling off the standard developmental milestones. She was doing her job perfectly, the way I do mine every single day. She looked at me, smiled, and said, "Well, you know all this, Herminia. You’re the pro."

In that moment, the tension was so sharp it almost took my breath away. I wanted to scream, or maybe just burst into tears. I wanted to say, "I don't want to be the pro. I want to be the terrified mom who gets to ask if it’s normal that she cries for three hours straight." But instead, I just smiled back and nodded. I recited the clinical response about her feeding intervals and sleep patterns, acting like everything was perfectly aligned.

Walking out to the car afterwards, I felt like a ghost. I had performed the role of the capable, knowledgeable nurse, but underneath, I was just a woman trying to figure out why I felt so utterly drained by the very things I teach others to master. It was the moment I realized my expertise had become a cage. I’m so busy being the expert that I’m forgetting how to be the person who’s allowed to just fall apart.

When you think about the support you received from your partner in the postpartum period — in caring for the baby, supporting your recovery, and sharing emotional labour — how would you describe that experience, and what would have made it better?

My husband, David, is a good man. He’s the one who takes the stroller out for walks when he sees me staring blankly at the wall, and he’s the one who tries to handle the late-night diapers so I can get a stretch of sleep. But there’s this unspoken dynamic where he defers to my "expertise" because I’m the medical professional. If the baby is fussy, he looks at me as if I have a diagnostic code for it. 

It’s exhausting. Sometimes I don’t want to be the one to decide the care plan; I want to just be a partner. When I try to voice that I’m drowning, he often pivots to practical solutions—"Should we change the schedule?" or "Did you call the doctor?"—which are meant to be helpful, but they feel like more work for me because I’m the one who has to manage the information.

What would have made it better? If he had just taken the mental load *off* me without me having to delegate it. I crave a kind of intuitive partnership where he just sees the mess—the emotional mess, not just the physical one—and holds it with me. I don’t need him to solve the "nursing problem" or the "infant sleep problem." I need him to see me, notice the heavy silence I’m carrying, and just sit in it with me, acknowledging that this is hard for us both.
How did the division of caregiving responsibilities compare to what you and your partner had discussed or assumed before the birth?

It’s funny, because we had so many "strategic planning" sessions during the pregnancy. We talked about feeding shifts, house chores, and who would handle the middle-of-the-night wakeups. We felt like a well-oiled machine before the baby even arrived. But the reality is that the division of labor has been almost entirely lopsided, mostly because of my own doing.

I think I assumed I would be able to manage the house and the baby with the same efficiency I bring to my clinic, and David assumed, because of my training, that I would naturally be the "lead" in our new domestic setup. He relies on me to set the tone, to know when the baby is hungry, or to decide what’s an "emergency" and what isn’t. 

I’m the one carrying the mental load, and while I’m resentful of it, I’m also the one who keeps picking up that weight. I’ve realized that I’m the one who sabotages the balance because I don’t fully trust anyone else to do it the way I think it *should* be done. It’s a toxic holdover from work. I’m exhausted by my own standards. I wanted us to be partners, but I’ve accidentally turned myself into a manager and him into an employee, and it’s a dynamic that is definitely not helping my recovery.

When you think about the barriers you encountered in accessing postnatal support — whether physical, emotional, informational, or systemic — what were the most significant, and what impact did they have on your recovery and wellbeing?

The biggest barrier was the assumption of my own competence—a systemic "blind spot" that I actually contribute to. Because I walk into a clinic and look like I have it together, the system effectively screens me *out* of support. The assumption is that if I’m not flagging a crisis, I don’t need a check-in. It’s a dangerous oversight, because it ignores the fact that high-functioning people often mask their struggles the hardest.

The impact has been a profound sense of isolation. I feel like I’m navigating a recovery that nobody is truly monitoring because they’re too busy nodding at my credentials. If I were a patient without my background, they might have been more patient, more probing, more present. But because I’m "one of them," I’m treated as someone who doesn't need the standard of care that—ironically—I know is vital for postpartum health.

It’s an informational barrier, too. There’s no space in the clinical routine for the "soft" stuff—for talking about the identity shift or the sheer exhaustion that isn’t just physical but soul-deep. The system is designed to catch medical complications, but it’s woefully equipped to catch the kind of quiet, mounting despair that comes from feeling like you’re failing at the very role you’re supposed to have mastered. I’m physically recovering, but emotionally, I feel like I’m on an island.
Can you describe a specific instance where you needed help but found it difficult to access — what happened and what got in the way?

A few weeks ago, I had a morning where the baby wouldn't latch, and the "expert" in me just snapped. I was sitting on the floor of the nursery, sobbing, holding my breast, and feeling this wave of pure, irrational failure. I knew I needed to talk to someone—not a peer, not a doctor—but just someone who would tell me I wasn't losing my mind. 

I picked up the phone to call the lactation consultant at my own clinic. I started dialing, but then I stopped. I imagined her voice, that professional, helpful tone she uses when we consult on patients, and I couldn't bear the thought of her hearing me cry. If I called, I’d have to explain that *I*—the one who teaches the classes—was the one failing at this. I was so terrified of being seen as the "hypocrite nurse" that I just put the phone down and sat in the dark for an hour instead.

What got in the way was my own ego and the professional reputation I’ve spent years building. I felt like by asking for help, I was admitting I didn't belong in the "expert" club anymore. I traded my mental well-being for a temporary, fragile sense of professional pride, and that moment of silence was honestly one of the heaviest I’ve ever lived through.

When you think about how you used digital tools, apps, social media, or online communities after birth — to seek information, find support, or track your recovery — what role did those resources play, and what were their limits?

I’m on both sides of the digital divide. I have the apps—the ones that track feedings, diapers, and growth curves. They feed that part of my brain that craves data, that need to feel like I’m in control of a situation that is inherently unpredictable. When I’m up at 3:00 AM, looking at a graph of the baby’s intake, it feels like "work," and for a minute, I feel competent again.

But the limits? They’re huge. Social media is the worst offender. I see these curated, peaceful images of mothers in the Salem area, looking pristine with their newborns in cozy knit sweaters, and the disconnect between that image and my reality—my leaking pads, my unwashed hair, the sheer terror I feel when the baby cries—is deafening. It sets this bar that I know is fake, yet I still find myself comparing my "performance" to those highlights.

Online communities feel like a trap, too. I read threads from other moms, and I find myself critiquing their logic or their advice from a clinical standpoint. It’s like I can’t turn off the "nurse" filter. It makes it impossible to just find human connection. I’m looking for community, but I keep finding things to analyze, which just pulls me further away from the raw, messy comfort I’m actually looking for.
Which specific digital tools or communities did you find most valuable, and what made them useful in ways that formal care was not?

There’s a small, private group of moms—some are colleagues, but most are just friends I’ve met through the school system—where the "professional" masks are mostly left at the door. That space is valuable because it isn't "clinical." When I text them at 2:00 AM, I don’t get a list of diagnostic criteria or "best practices" in response. I get a string of laughing emojis, a "me too," or someone admitting that they, too, forgot to brush their teeth for two days.

What makes this space useful is the lack of hierarchy. In formal care, there is always an expert and a patient. In these informal groups, we’re all just "people." When I say I’m struggling, they don’t ask for my stats. They just validate the mess. It’s the difference between being told how to handle a problem and being told that the problem is a shared, human experience.

Formal care is great for the "what," but it’s completely hollow for the "how." These digital threads are where I get to be a human being, not a clinician. It’s the one place where I don’t have to prove I know the material. I can just admit that I’m tired, that I’m crying, and that I’m completely overwhelmed, and nobody tries to "treat" me. They just bear witness. That’s something I haven't found in my clinical appointments at all.

When you reflect on your entire maternity journey — from preconception through to the postpartum period — what do you wish had been done differently by the care system, and what single change would have made the greatest difference to your experience?

I think I would have thrived if someone had explicitly "de-professionalized" my care from the start. I wish my midwife or my primary care provider had sat me down in that very first visit and said, "Herminia, I know what you do for a living, but today, you are the patient. We are leaving your clinical brain at the door. I am going to ask you questions, and I want you to answer from your heart, not from your training."

That simple permission—to be incompetent, to be scared, to not know the answers—would have been a lifeline. The greatest difference would have been a proactive, non-clinical check-in. Not an appointment where we go over the chart or the checklist, but a conversation that focuses purely on the emotional transition. 

If the system had acknowledged that my expertise is a source of pressure rather than a shield, I think I would have been much more honest about my struggles. I wouldn’t have had to carry the burden of "performing" as a mother. I just wanted someone to acknowledge that even if you can teach a woman how to latch her baby, it doesn’t mean you’re immune to the crushing, raw, beautiful exhaustion of actually doing it yourself. I needed a caregiver, not an audience.
Can you describe the specific moment or phase where you felt most let down by the system — what was the gap and what would have helped?

It was at my six-week check-up. The room was bright, the nurse was efficient, and the midwife was friendly, but it was just a series of clinical benchmarks: checking my healing, asking about contraception, and reviewing the baby’s weight gain. 

I sat there, my hands shaking slightly—partly from exhaustion, partly from the sheer weight of trying to keep up this professional mask—and I kept waiting for someone to ask, "How are *you* doing, really? Not the nurse, but the mother?" But the question never came. Because I was "low-risk" and a colleague, they skipped the deeper, more vulnerable mental health screenings they might have given another patient. They treated my competence as a barrier to my care.

The gap was a complete lack of human-centric, empathetic space. What would have helped? Simply having the practitioner acknowledge the transition. Acknowledging that because I work in this field, I might be under *more* pressure to appear perfect, not less. A simple "I know this is a hard shift, even for someone who knows the biology" would have shattered the facade and allowed me to breathe. I left that appointment feeling like I had succeeded as a professional, but failed as a person who needed, and deserved, to be held.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

You know, there’s one more thing that feels important. We talk so much about "maternal health" as if it’s just about recovery—healing from the physical, navigating the hormones, and surviving the exhaustion. But there’s this specific, piercing kind of grief I didn't see coming. It’s the grief of watching your professional identity and your "newborn-mother" identity try to exist in the same body, and realizing they don't quite fit together. 

I think the system treats the "professional mother" as a unicorn—someone who’s got it all figured out, who’s resilient and ready to bounce back to the clinic floor. But I’m not a unicorn. I’m just a woman who’s tired of being the person with the answers. 

I’d like the people in charge of maternity care to know that when a clinician walks into your office as a patient, you are actually sitting across from someone who is arguably at higher risk for burnout and silent suffering. We are trained to suppress our own needs to serve others. If you see a colleague coming in for her own care, please, *please* challenge her to take that white coat off. Force her to sit in the discomfort of being the one who doesn't know. That’s where the real care happens. That’s what would have saved me.
